text: Different from its function of promoting bone formation in osteoblasts, cancer cell-derived RUNX2 was found to have an adverse effect on cancer-related bone destruction. As reported, RUNX2 facilitates bone resorption by regulating the expression of TGFβ, MMP-9, ITGA5, or RANKL [ 28 – 31 ]. While most studies on RUNX2 and bone destruction focus on the influence on osteoclasts, the interaction between cancer cell-derived RUNX2 and osteoblasts is not well understood. By extracting CCM and applying it to both osteoclasts and osteoblasts, we found that RUNX2 upregulation in myeloma cells not only enhanced the promoting effect of myeloma cells on osteoclast activity but also the suppressive effect of myeloma cells on osteoblast activity and facilitated the differentiation of osteoclasts mediated by osteoblasts, ultimately aggravating bone destruction.
summary: In myeloma cells, cancer cell–derived RUNX2 promotes bone destruction. Using conditioned culture medium on osteoclasts and osteoblasts, RUNX2 upregulation in myeloma cells enhanced myeloma-driven osteoclast activity and increased myeloma-driven suppression of osteoblast activity. RUNX2 also facilitated osteoblast-mediated differentiation of osteoclasts. RUNX2 promoted bone resorption by regulating TGFβ, MMP-9, ITGA5, or RANKL and ultimately aggravated bone destruction.

text: In conclusion, we propose that upregulated RUNX2 promotes the suppression of osteoblast activity and enhancement of osteoclast activity by myeloma cells. Additional studies are needed to determine the role of M-CSF and LCN2 in RUNX2-related bone destruction. Further investigation of the interaction between M-CSF, LCN2, and osteoclasts or osteoblasts is needed. The study lays the foundation for further research and suggests that therapeutic inhibition of RUNX2 may protect against bone destruction by maintaining the balance between osteoblast and osteoclast activity in MM.
summary: In multiple myeloma, upregulated RUNX2 in myeloma cells promotes suppression of osteoblast activity. It also promotes enhancement of osteoclast activity. These changes contribute to bone destruction. Therapeutic inhibition of RUNX2 may protect against bone destruction by maintaining the balance between osteoblast and osteoclast activity in MM. The roles of M-CSF and LCN2 in RUNX2-related bone destruction remain to be determined.

text: In this study, we show that increased expression of RUNX2 is responsible for bone destruction in MM. We demonstrate that RUNX2 promotes the suppression of osteoblast activity and enhancement of osteoclast activity by multiple myeloma cells using in vitro and in vivo approaches. Importantly, our data provide evidence that therapeutic inhibition of RUNX2 may protect against bone destruction by maintaining the balance between osteoblast and osteoclast activity in MM.
summary: In multiple myeloma, increased expression of RUNX2 is responsible for bone destruction. RUNX2 promotes suppression of osteoblast activity and enhancement of osteoclast activity by multiple myeloma cells in vitro and in vivo. Therapeutic inhibition of RUNX2 may protect against bone destruction by maintaining the balance between osteoblast and osteoclast activity in MM.
